text:
The way law is practiced and governed is changing in response to the pressures of globalization, an imperative for greater efficiency, and the emerging technology that is transforming the way we live and work. This third edition of Tomorrow’s Lawyers, the highly acclaimed critique of the legal profession, is again written by the leading voice on technological change in the law. Building on the first two editions, the author asserts that the bespoke, case-by-case model of legal practice is being replaced by a more efficiency-driven approach, in which legal services are delivered more quickly, economically and with the greater use of technology. At the heart of the necessary transformation are the three challenges facing the profession: the more-for-less challenge (the need to maintain standards while reducing charges); the liberalization challenge (opening up the justice system to new entrants and different models of law practice); and the technology challenge (the embedding of artificial intelligence and other systems in legal work and its associated changes to the way that legal services are delivered and how lawyers work.

This book describes what the next generation of lawyers will have to do, to be successful in a rapidly changing legal world, in detail for the first time. It describes new law business models and even the workings of virtual courts and moves from talking about the need for what is referred to as “compliance counselling” – (pre-emptive) legal risk management as opposed to the traditional reactive approach to law, in which lawyers respond to developments that have already given rise to risk. The future lawyers will not be just experts in law – a change in function that will see a shift from what have been called “law specialists” to legal process delegates, knowledge engineers and, sometimes, legal technologists. Susskind suggests that a more modern, and in particular, a more accessible and cheaper system of law and justice, is possible, and challenges practising lawyers and law students to consider what is needed to bring about a legal world that has fully and properly adapted to the technological revolution which has enabled legal work and legal expertise to be provided by a series of efficient and highly-cost effective systems – in place of the old charges for time.
